*   in error processing (RSNAST0F) if there is any syntax error / update
*   termination / ... in the decoupled NAST processing.
  returncode = 4.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.                                                    "^_n_1238590

returncode = 0.
* Get Item Data of Delivery
*  ls_leshp_data-item = 'X'.                                  "n_1822545
* Get Status of Outbound Delivery
ls_leshp_data-head_status = 'X'.
* Copy Nast Entry
gs_nast = nast.
ls_vbeln_vl = nast-objky.

CALL FUNCTION 'LE_DELIVERY_GET_BUFFERED'
  EXPORTING
    if_vbeln               = ls_vbeln_vl
    is_data                = ls_leshp_data
  CHANGING
    cx_deliveries          = lt_delivery_data
  EXCEPTIONS
    no_item_selected       = 1
    selected_item_enqueued = 2
    no_key_specified       = 3
    OTHERS                 = 4.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE e267(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
  returncode = sy-subrc.
ELSE.

* only one delivery can be found by premisses (VBELN).
  READ TABLE lt_delivery_data INDEX 1 INTO ls_delivery_data.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    IF ls_delivery_data-head_status-fkstk = c_allready_billed.
* Allready billed.
      returncode = 0.
      MESSAGE i265(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
    ELSE.
*        lt_delivery_items = ls_delivery_data-item.         "v_n_1822545
*        LOOP AT lt_delivery_items ASSIGNING <ls_billing_req>.
*          MOVE-CORRESPONDING <ls_billing_req> TO ls_doc_pos_ver_req.
*          APPEND ls_doc_pos_ver_req TO lt_doc_pos_ver_req.
*        ENDLOOP.
      ls_doc_pos_ver_req-vbeln = ls_delivery_data-head-vbeln.
      APPEND ls_doc_pos_ver_req TO lt_doc_pos_ver_req.    "^_n_1822545

      CALL FUNCTION '/SPE/CREATE_NEW_BILLING_CALL'
        EXPORTING
          it_billing_req     = lt_doc_pos_ver_req
          iv_version_checked = 'X'
        IMPORTING
          et_return          = lt_bapiret2.
      LOOP AT lt_bapiret2 ASSIGNING <ls_return>.
        IF <ls_return>-type = 'E'.
          lv_error = 'X'.
        ENDIF.
      ENDLOOP.
      IF lv_error = 'X'.
        returncode = 1.
        use_screen = ' '.
      ELSE.
        returncode = 0.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

  ELSE.
* No Entry found !!!
    MESSAGE e267(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
    returncode = 9.
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.
CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_INITIALIZE'.
LOOP AT lt_bapiret2 ASSIGNING <ls_return>.
  IF <ls_return>-type = 'E'.
    lv_error = 'X'.
  ENDIF.
  PERFORM protocol_update_return USING <ls_return>.
ENDLOOP.
IF lv_error = 'X'.
  returncode = 1.
  use_screen = ' '.
ELSE.
  returncode = 0.
ENDIF.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  PERFORM protocol_update.
ENDIF.
ENDFORM.                    " ENTRY

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       FORM PROTOCOL_UPDATE                                          *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       The messages are collected for the processing protocol.       *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*

FORM protocol_update.

  CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      msg_arbgb = syst-msgid
      msg_nr    = syst-msgno
      msg_ty    = syst-msgty
      msg_v1    = syst-msgv1
      msg_v2    = syst-msgv2
      msg_v3    = syst-msgv3
      msg_v4    = syst-msgv4
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS    = 1.

ENDFORM.                    "PROTOCOL_UPDATE
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  protocol_update_return
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*      -->P_<LS_RETURN>  text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM protocol_update_return USING p_return TYPE bapiret2.

  CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      msg_arbgb = p_return-id
      msg_nr    = p_return-number
      msg_ty    = p_return-type
      msg_v1    = p_return-message_v1
      msg_v2    = p_return-message_v2
      msg_v3    = p_return-message_v3
      msg_v4    = p_return-message_v4
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS    = 1.

ENDFORM.                    " protocol_update_return

7. Final Architecture Principle

Standard-first approach

Low-coding strategy

Dynamic control via enhancement

Output-driven automation

Controlled customer-based exclusion

Lock-safe execution

8. Conclusion

This solution delivers a fully automated Order → Delivery → Goods Issue → Billing process while maintaining:

  • High adherence to SAP standards
  • Minimal custom development footprint
  • Flexible customer-level control
  • System stability and scalability

By combining standard configuration (VOV8, VV21), enhancement logic (MV45AFZZ), and output-based processing (REA0), an end-to-end automated and maintainable SD process was successfully implemented.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: CPFH Sales Kits in Consignment
entry.content:
Introduction

With release 2402, support for Sales Kits of type CPFH in consignment processing was delivered.

At first sight, this may look like a limited scope enhancement: allowing a Sales Kit to be used in consignment scenarios. In reality, the change is much more significant. The real requirement was not just order entry support, but a process model that preserves the commercial meaning of the kit while still allowing the system to execute the logistics and inventory steps on component level.

This is exactly where CPFH becomes relevant.

In a CPFH Sales Kit, the processing logic is clearly split:

  • the main item carries the commercial meaning and pricing
  • the subitems carry the delivery- and inventory-relevant execution

This split is particularly important in consignment processing, because consignment is not just a sales-document-driven scenario. It is a stock-oriented business process with distinct follow-up steps:

  • Consignment Fill-Up
  • Consignment Issue
  • Consignment Pick-Up
  • Consignment Return

For a normal material, these steps are straightforward. For a Sales Kit, they are not. The customer orders one commercial object, but the system needs to execute on multiple physical components.

This blog explains how CPFH Sales Kits are processed in consignment, what the business meaning of the relevant item categories is, and how the solution integrates into the standard consignment execution framework.

Business Background

Among the different Sales Kit types, CPFH is especially suitable for consignment because it already reflects the separation that consignment processing needs:

  • commercial meaning on header level
  • execution meaning on component level

From a business perspective, this allows the system to preserve the kit as the object the customer sees and buys.

From an execution perspective, it allows the system to process the components that are physically delivered, consumed, picked up, or returned.

Without such a split, a kit might still look correct in the sales document, but the downstream process would become inconsistent. The document would show one object, while the actual stock movements would need another.

The core design principle is therefore simple:

The header keeps the kit semantics. The subitems carry the consignment execution semantics.

Scope Delivered in 2402

The current delivery scope focuses on Sales Kits of type CPFH for the following consignment process steps:

  1. Consignment Fill-Up
  2. Consignment Issue
  3. Consignment Pick-Up
  4. Consignment Return

Across all four steps, the solution distinguishes between:

  • header item categories, which define the business role of the kit header
  • subitem item categories, which define the execution role of the components

This distinction is essential. The different item categories are not technical duplicates. They exist because the business meaning of the kit header is not the same in Fill-Up, Issue, Pick-Up, and Return.

Overall Processing Logic

The following table summarizes the end-to-end logic of CPFH in consignment.

Table 1: CPFH in Consignment – Overall Logic

Business Step Header Item Category Header Meaning Subitem Item Category Subitem Meaning Schedule Line Movement Type
Fill-UpCF3HKit header, preserves kit semantics, not used as a normal revenue-relevant billing itemCF3IComponents moved into customer consignment stockE1 / E0631
IssueCPHDPriced and billing-relevant kit headerCI3LComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
IssueCFHDNot priced on header, but still billing-relevantCI3LComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
IssueCNHDNon-priced, non-billing header, used only to preserve kit structureCI3FComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
Pick-UpCP3HKit header for pick-up processingCP3IComponents picked up from customer consignment stockF1 / F0632
ReturnCR3PPriced and billing-relevant return headerCR3LComponents processed in returnD0634
ReturnCR3FNot priced on header, but still billing-relevantCR3LComponents processed in returnD0634
ReturnCR3NNon-priced, non-billing header, used only to preserve kit structureCR3IComponents processed in returnD0634

This table highlights the key principle of the solution:

  • the header categories differentiate the business role of the kit header
  • the subitem categories carry the actual execution logic
  • the stock-relevant processing always happens on subitem level

Consignment Fill-Up

Business Meaning

In Consignment Fill-Up, stock is moved to customer consignment stock.
At this stage, the customer has not yet actually consumed the goods. Therefore, Fill-Up is not a revenue event. It is much closer to a stock transfer into customer consignment stock.

That business meaning is reflected in the item category design.

Header: CF3H

The CPFH header item category in Fill-Up is CF3H.

Its purpose is to:

  • preserve the Sales Kit header in the document
  • keep the commercial representation of the kit
  • provide the structural basis for the exploded subitems

At the same time, CF3H is not the execution-relevant stock item. And because Fill-Up is not the point of revenue realization, it is not intended for normal billing logic. In practice, it fits pro forma invoice semantics rather than standard billing.

So the role of CF3H is not to execute the stock movement, but to keep the kit visible and meaningful in the process.

Subitem: CF3I

The actual execution in Fill-Up happens on CF3I.

CF3I represents:

  • the exploded component line
  • the material actually moved into customer consignment stock
  • the execution-relevant line for Fill-Up

It is mapped to:

  • E1 / E0
  • movement type 631

So the Fill-Up logic can be summarized as:

CF3H keeps the kit header. CF3I performs the actual fill-up execution.

Consignment Issue

Issue is the most commercially relevant step in consignment, because this is the point where the customer actually consumes stock from customer consignment inventory.

This is why the system distinguishes between multiple header item categories for Issue. They are not redundant. They reflect different business meanings of the kit header.

CPHD – Priced and Billing-Relevant Issue Header

CPHD represents the most direct commercial Issue path.

Its meaning is:

  • the header is priced
  • the header is billing-relevant
  • the kit has entered a commercial settlement path

The corresponding execution-relevant subitem is CI3L.

So CPHD + CI3L represents:

A commercially relevant kit header combined with component-level issue execution.

CFHD – Not Priced on Header, but Still Billing-Relevant

CFHD differs from CPHD.

Here, the header does not behave like a standard priced header item, but it still remains billing-relevant. This makes CFHD an alternative Issue path where commercial relevance is preserved, but header pricing is not the defining characteristic.

The corresponding subitem is also CI3L.

CNHD – Non-Priced and Non-Billing Issue Header

CNHD is especially important to understand.

Its role is:

  • no pricing
  • no billing
  • the header exists only to preserve the structural semantics of the kit

This means CNHD is not a commercial settlement header. It is a structural header. The document still needs a valid kit header, even though the actual execution happens on the components.

The corresponding subitem is CI3F.

So CNHD + CI3F represents:

A purely structural Issue header with actual component-level execution.

Subitems in Issue: CI3L and CI3F

The actual Issue execution is always done on the subitems, not on the header.

The relevant subitem categories are:

  • CI3L
  • CI3F

Both are mapped to:

  • C1 / C0
  • movement type 633

Their common role is clear:

They carry the actual component-level consumption of consignment stock.

Consignment Pick-Up

Consignment Pick-Up retrieves stock from customer consignment stock that has not yet been consumed.

Again, the business document keeps the kit header, but execution happens on the components.

Header: CP3H

CP3H is the CPFH header item category for Pick-Up.

Its purpose is to:

  • preserve the kit structure in the document
  • keep the kit header visible in pick-up processing
  • provide the structural basis for the component lines

Subitem: CP3I

The actual Pick-Up execution happens on CP3I.

It represents:

  • the components being retrieved
  • the stock-relevant line in pick-up processing

It is mapped to:

  • F1 / F0
  • movement type 632

So Pick-Up follows the same overall principle:

CP3H preserves the kit meaning, while CP3I performs the actual component-level retrieval.

Consignment Return

Return processing again differentiates between multiple header paths, because not every return header has the same commercial meaning.

CR3P – Priced and Billing-Relevant Return Header

CR3P represents:

  • a priced header
  • a billing-relevant return path
  • a commercially relevant return header for the kit

The corresponding subitem is CR3L.

So CR3P + CR3L means:

A commercially relevant return header with actual component-level return execution.

CR3F – Not Priced on Header, but Still Billing-Relevant

CR3F is conceptually similar to CFHD in Issue.

Its meaning is:

  • the header is not priced in the normal way
  • but it remains billing-relevant

The corresponding subitem is also CR3L.

CR3N – Non-Priced and Non-Billing Return Header

CR3N is the Return counterpart of CNHD.

Its role is:

  • no pricing
  • no billing
  • the header exists only to preserve the structural semantics of the kit

The corresponding execution-relevant subitem is CR3I.

So CR3N + CR3I means:

A purely structural return header with actual component-level return execution.

Subitems in Return: CR3L and CR3I

The actual Return execution happens on:

  • CR3L
  • CR3I

These are mapped to:

  • D0
  • movement type 634

So even in Return, the business pattern remains unchanged:

The header keeps the kit meaning, while the subitem performs the actual return execution.

Relationship to Standard Consignment Item Categories

A helpful way to understand the design is to compare the CPFH subitems with the standard consignment item categories.

Table 2: Standard vs. CPFH Execution Paths

Standard Item Category Business Step CPFH Subitem Item Category Meaning
CFNFill-UpCF3IStandard fill-up item vs. CPFH component fill-up item
CIN / CCIN / CCXNIssueCI3L / CI3FStandard issue item vs. CPFH component issue item
CPNPick-UpCP3IStandard pick-up item vs. CPFH component pick-up item
CRN / CRBN / CCRNReturnCR3L / CR3IStandard return item vs. CPFH component return item

This comparison makes one design principle very clear:

The solution does not create a new consignment execution model. It adds a header/subitem split on top of the standard consignment execution patterns.

In other words:

  • for a normal material, the system directly uses the standard item category path
  • for a CPFH kit, the system keeps the kit header and lets the subitems reuse the standard execution behavior

Technical Design Perspective

From a technical point of view, the implementation can be understood in three layers.

1. Item Category Determination

The system first determines the header item category, and then derives the subitem item category based on the higher-level item category.

This allows the document to preserve the kit structure while still generating execution-relevant component lines.

2. Business Role of Header and Subitem

The header item categories distinguish whether the kit header is:

  • priced
  • billing-relevant
  • or purely structural

The subitem item categories distinguish the actual execution role of the components for:

  • Fill-Up
  • Issue
  • Pick-Up
  • Return

3. Schedule Line Determination and Movement Execution

The subitems are then mapped back to the standard consignment execution framework:

  • Fill-Up: E1 / E0 → 631
  • Issue: C1 / C0 → 633
  • Pick-Up: F1 / F0 → 632
  • Return: D0 → 634

So the real innovation of the solution is not a new movement model. The innovation is:

making the CPFH kit structure executable within the standard consignment framework.

Roadmap Outlook

With the delivery of CPFH support in consignment processing in 2402, the first step has been taken to enable Sales Kits in this area.

Support for additional Sales Kit types, including ERLA, DISH, and LUMF, is planned to be introduced gradually in future releases after 2702, further expanding the overall coverage of Sales Kits in consignment scenarios.

Conclusion

Supporting CPFH in consignment is not just about allowing a Sales Kit in a consignment order. It addresses a more fundamental business and technical requirement:

How can a Sales Kit remain a kit from a commercial perspective, while still being executed consistently on component level in consignment processing?

The answer is based on three principles:

  1. The header keeps the kit semantics
    Different header item categories distinguish whether the header is priced, billing-relevant, or purely structural.
  2. The subitems carry the execution semantics
    The real inventory and movement-relevant processing happens on the component-level subitems.
  3. The execution layer reuses the standard consignment framework
    Standard schedule line categories and movement types are reused, so CPFH can run consistently within existing consignment processing.

With the 2402 delivery, CPFH Sales Kits can now be processed in consignment while preserving both:

  • the commercial representation of the kit
  • the execution logic of the components

This also lays the groundwork for further support of additional Sales Kit types in consignment scenarios in future releases.

entry.title: How the condition value (XKOMV-KWERT) is calculated in subtotal pricing conditions
entry.content:
It is not always clear for some end users how the subtotal condition value is calculated.
In the following blog post, I will explain it in details.

Different from what is observed for pricing conditions, subtotal value is calculated in a different way.

For example, in the below scenario, we observe that subtotal 'Net value + Tax' has condition value calculated as '10.559,05', but most of the users would expect its calculation as '58,99 * 179 = 10.559,21', considering the quantity as '179':





According to note 0001007110, mainly the following part, subtotal value is calculated in the following way:

...
This consulting note describes how the condition value (the XKOMV-KWERT field) of a subtotal (lines of the pricing procedure without a condition type) is determined in the pricing result.
...

2. One or more reference steps are maintained:

The condition value of the subtotal is determined in the same way as the condition basis is determined by 'value-related' conditions.
See Note 0000834174, section 2.
...


Then, from note 0000834174 - section 2:

...

2. One or more reference steps are maintained:

Only step numbers that are smaller than the current reference step can be chosen as reference steps. This is because the condition values of following step numbers have not yet been determined due to the successive processing of the pricing procedure (see above).

If only the from-reference step is maintained, the system internally fills the to-reference step with the from-reference step. In this case therefore only this one reference step is relevant.
If both the from-reference step and the to-reference step (greater or equal to the from-reference step) are maintained, all step numbers are relevant, that are greater than or equal to the from-reference step and smaller than or equal to the to-reference step.
To determine the condition basis, the system now adds the condition values of all relevant conditions that are not inactive with an 'AKLMX' indicator (see Note 836243).
It should be noted that in this case pricing conditions with the inactivity indicator 'Y' are taken into account during the determination of the condition basis, as long as their step number is in the relevant area.
In addition, the system also takes subtotals into account, as long as their step number is in the relevant area.
...

Therefore, when checking the related pricing procedure, we see that subtotal 'Net value + Tax' has 'from/to' steps referring interval of '10 to 80', which refers to pricing conditions 'PR00' and 'MWST', which are the existent ones in the example above.



Hence, subtotal condition value (KWERT) for 'Net value + Tax' will be calculated as 'PR00 + MWST', resulting in '9.868,27 + 690,78 = 10.599,05'.

Technically speaking, this takes place in the logic below:

Include LV61AA55
Form XKOMV_BEWERTEN 
...
      ELSE.
        IF xkomv-kschl EQ space.
          PERFORM xkomv_kwert_ermitteln.
        ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
...


Now, to calculate the condition amount (KBETR) for subtotals, system performs a backward calculation, as explained in note 0000791944, i.e.:

...
2. In general, the condition rate of a subtotal is determined by dividing the condition value of the subtotal (see Note 1007110) by the condition basis of the pricing condition (YKMENG) ("backward calculation").
...


Then, for subtotal 'Net value + Tax', condition rate will be calculated as '10.599,05 / 179 = 58,99'.


Technically speaking:

Include LV61AA43
Form XKOMV_KWERT_ERMITTELN
...
* price per unit if sum line
  IF xkomv-kschl = space.
    PERFORM xkomv_kbetr_from_kwert USING xkomv-kwert.
  ENDIF.
...


If a different behavior is required for calculating the subtotal condition rate or value, a custom formula should be developed.

I hope this clarifies this recurrent topic.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Clean Core e VOFM
entry.content:

https://lnkd.in/dQ4HksK4

E para os que não vivem sem um desenvolvimento na pricing, segue a nota:
